Variables act as labeled storage boxes. When a code is written, there should be a way to hold onto information, like a user’s name, a shopping cart total, or whether a menu is open. In javascript, that information is packed into boxes called variables and is labeled.
Variable Declaration:
In JavaScript, variables are declared in three different ways:

• var (The Legacy: This is the old way of doing things. It has quirky scoping rules that often cause bugs, so modern JavaScript developers generally avoid it.
var legacyServerSetting = "Region-US";
• const (The Default): Use this for values that should never change. If you try to reassign a const variable, JavaScript will throw an error.
const playerId = "Player_X99";
• let (The Variable): Use this when you expect the value to change later (like a game score or a loop counter).
let currentScore = 0;
